reasoning: The resolution criteria require the U.S. government to implement *new or expanded* export controls explicitly targeting frontier AI model development with an effective date by 31 July 2026. Following the rescission of the Biden-era AI Diffusion Rule in May 2025, the Trump administration has consistently moved toward deregulation over strict export controls to promote U.S. "AI supremacy." On January 15, 2026, BIS finalized a rule that actually *relaxed* license review policies for advanced chips entering China and Macau, shifting from a presumption of denial to a case-by-case review. In early March 2026, there were internal deliberations over a new rule requiring countries to make U.S. data center investments or provide security guarantees for exports of over 200,000 AI chips. However, on March 13, 2026, the Commerce Department officially withdrew this planned rule following internal divisions and backpedaling. With the administration characterizing previous restriction frameworks as "burdensome, overreaching, and disastrous," and having just withdrawn their only major draft rule targeting large AI clusters less than five months before the July 31 cutoff, the likelihood of drafting, proposing, and implementing a new prohibitive export control rule within the timeframe is extremely low.
